English: No, no, no, no err, a river here for, for to [?]come in.

Speaker: WC
Manx: Oh ta ram ayns baie Rhumsaa.
English: Oh there are lots in Ramsey bay.

Speaker: WC
Manx: Ta ram jeu ayns baie Rhumsaa.
English: There are lots of them in Ramsey bay.

Speaker: WC
Manx: Ayns y sourey, choud's ta mee toiggal. Ta.
English: In the summer, as far as I understand. Yes.

Speaker: NM
Manx: Vel ad goll seose ayns y, ayns y, ayns yn awin?
English: Are they going up in the, in the, in the river?

Speaker: NM
Manx: Oh veagh ad, bee ad goll seose ayns err, err, Balley Cashtal as, as err ...
English: Oh they would be, they will be going up in err, err, Castletown and, and err ...

Speaker: NM
Manx: ... as Doolish neesht, as Cass ny Hawin.

Speaker: DF
Manx: Doolish. Cass ny Hawin, shen yn boayl share t'ad gra.
English: Douglas. Cass ny Hawin, that's the best place they say.

Speaker: WC
Manx: Oh va mee magh ec Kione ny Hayrey lesh lieen, eeastagh braddanyn dy mennick.
English: Oh I was out at the Point of Ayre with a net, fishing salmon often.

Speaker: NM
Manx: Ah t'ad ooilley ro deyr jiu son dy, son dy kionnagh ad.
English: Ah they are all too expensive to, to buy them.

Speaker: NM
Manx: Cha jean yn, yn argid greie eh.

Speaker: DF
Manx: As c'red ta shiu coontey jeh'n emshyr jiu?
English: And what do you think of the weather today?

Speaker: NM
Manx: Cha nel mee coontey monney jeh.
English: I don't think much of it.

Speaker: NM
Manx: Ta shin goll dy geddyn caghlaa.
English: We are going to get a change.

Speaker: NM
Manx: Ta shin goll dy geddyn palchey geay as fliaghey.
English: We are going to get plenty of wind and rain.

Speaker: NM
Manx: Oh, c'red, n- cha nel shen dy liooar dhyt?
English: Oh, what, that's not enough for you?

Speaker: DF
Manx: Ta ram bodjallyn mygeayrt neesht, nagh vel?
English: There are lots of clouds about too, aren't there?

Speaker: NM
Manx: Oh my, ta, ta'n, ta'n, ta'n, ta'n aer bunnys coodit.
English: Oh yes, the, the, the, the sky is nearly covered.

Speaker: NM
Manx: Ta. Cha ren mee fakin, well, va, va, ren mee fakin yn, yn, yn, yn eayst, yn err, grian moghree shoh. Agh, cha row eh jeeaghyn mie.
English: Yes. I didn't see, well, there was, was, I saw the, the, the, the, the moon, the err, sun this morning. But, it wasn't looking good.

Speaker: DF
Manx: Ta shen drogh cowrey nagh vel?
English: That's a bad sign isn't it?

Speaker: NM
Manx: Ta, ta, ta, ta, ta, ta, ta.
English: Yes, yes, yes, yes, yes, yes, yes.

Speaker: NM
Manx: Va peiagh ennagh briaght jeem, nane jeh ny laghyn, laghyn chaie, dy yeeaghyn, row, row, row ad freayll yn, yn, yn Gailck goll foast, as dooyrt mish dy row, as dooyrt, as dooyrt yn fer "Quoi ta gra, quoi ta taggloo eh eisht?" as dooyrt mish "Fer enmyssit Faragher, as fer elley enmyssit Radcliffe, voish Rhumsaa."
English: Someone was asking me, one of the days, days recently, to see, whether they were keeping the, the, the Manx going still, and I said that they were, and the fellow said, said "Who is saying, who is speaking it then?" and I said "A fellow called Faragher, and another fellow called Radcliffe, from Ramsey."

Speaker: NM
Manx: V'eh goaill yindys, dy row ad, dy, dy row, row deiney aeg abyl taggloo eh.
English: He was amazed, that they were, that threre were, were young men able to speak it.

Speaker: NM
English: I don't know who it was.

Speaker: NM
Manx: Cha row eh, cha row eh joarree, err, err y dooinney va mygeayrt ...
English: He wasn't, he wasn't a foreigner, err, err the man who was around ...

Speaker: DF
Manx: Oh t'eh ny smoo na, ny smoo na shinyn neesht, t'eh ...
English: Oh it's more than, more than us too, it's ...

Speaker: WR
Manx: Oh she, foddey ny smoo na shinyn.
English: Oh she, far more than us.

Speaker: DF
Manx: T'eh mysh err, mysh three feed dy licklee. Three feed [?]deiney aegey.
English: It's about err, about sixty probably. Sixty young men.

Speaker: NM
Manx: She? Oh ta, ta. Agh cha nel mish, cha nel ad cheet yn raad aym's my ta.
English: Yes? Oh yes, yes. But I am not, they don't come my way though.

Speaker: NM
Manx: Well cha nel err, cha nel fys aym's c'red t'ad gra eisht, ...
English: Well I don't err, I don't know what they say then, ...

Speaker: NM
Manx: ... tra nagh vel err, tra nagh vel mee taggloo roo.
English: ... when I err, when I don't talk to them.

Speaker: DF
Manx: Oh nee mayd, nee mayd freayll eh bio, choud as ta shin abyl.
English: Oh we'll, we'll keep it alive, as long as we can.

Speaker: NM
Manx: Ah? Choud as t'ou bio oo hene.
English: Ah? As long as you are alive yourself.

Speaker: DF
Manx: As foddee bee ny paitchyn ain hene, bee adsyn abyl dy loayrt Gailck neesht.
English: And maybe our own children, they will be able to speak Manx too.

Speaker: NM
Manx: Err, err. Oh foddee dy bee, foddee dy bee!
English: Err, err. Oh maybe they will, maybe they will!

Speaker: NM
Manx: T'ou jeeaghyn myr lhisagh oo, ceau son tammylt liauyr ansh erbee.
English: You look like you should, last for a long while anyway.

Speaker: WR
Manx: Oh nee eshyn ceau son tammylt foast!
English: Oh he will last for a while yet!

Speaker: DF
Manx: Cha nel mee jeeaghyn ro olk my ta.
English: I don't look too bad so I don't.

Speaker: NM
Manx: Cha nel, cha nel, cha nel, cha nel.

Speaker: DF
Manx: Va fer ennagh ayns Doolish laa jea as dooyrt eh yn red cheddin, dy vel mee jeeaghyn mie agglagh.
English: There was a fellow in Douglas yesterday and he said the same thing, that I am looking awfully well.

Speaker: NM
English: Who was he? Who was he?

Speaker: NM
Manx: Row eh, row eh, row eh voish y Jiass?
English: Was he, was he, was he from the South?

Speaker: NM
Manx: T'ou jeeaghyn mie neesht, as gow kiarail jeed hene. Ta deiney mie feer goan nish.
English: You look well too, and take care of yourself. Good men are very scarce now.

Speaker: DF
English: Oh yes, yes, they are scarce.

Speaker: DF
Manx: T'ad goll-rish ny, ny partanyn, [?]dagh laa va shiuish goll trooid baie Purt Chiarn un keayrt.
English: They are like the, the crabs, [?]each day you were going through Port Erin bay on time.

Speaker: NM
Manx: "Vel ny partanyn snaue Joe err Joe?" dooyrt eh. [laughs]
English: "Are the crabs crawling Joe, err, Joe?" he said. [laughs]

Speaker: DF
Manx: Va shen skeeal mie. Well ta'n. ta'n red shoh bunnys jeant.
English: That was a good story. Well this thing is nearly done.

Speaker: NM
Manx: Oh well. Ah well, ta, ta, ta dy liooar grait ain nish.
English: Oh well. Ah well, we have said enough now.

Speaker: DF
Manx: Oh cha nel, cha bee dy liooar grait ayd ...
English: Oh no, you won't have said enough ...

Speaker: WR
Manx: Cha nel yn kierroo raait ta shin laccal.
English: Not a quarter has been said of what we want.

Speaker: NM
Manx: Agh ta shin goll dy yannoo yn, yn, yn, yn un red ta shin goll ...
English: But we are going to do the, the, the, the same thing that we have ...

Speaker: DF
Manx: As va shen, ta shen mie yindyssagh.
English: And that was, that was wonderfully good.

Speaker: DF
Manx: Shen ta shin, ta shin laccal son ...
English: That's what we, what we want for ...

Speaker: NM
Manx: Son goll [?]eisht, ta shin, bee shin goll harrish yn un red reesht, eisht.
English: For going [?]then, we are, we will be going over the same thing again, then.

Speaker: DF
Manx: Ta ram, ram reddyn faagit ayns y kione ayd foast.
English: There are lots, lots of things left in your head still.

Speaker: NM
Manx: Oh cha nel dy jarroo. Oh t'ou er n'eddyn ooilley ny t'aym's.
English: Oh no indeed. Oh you have got all that I have.

Speaker: DF
Manx: Tra v'ad, v'ad sneeu, v'ad sneeu ollan ayns Creneash as reddyn myr shen.
English: When they were, they were spinning, they were spinning wool in Creneash and things like that.

Speaker: NM
English: Oh they were doing that right ...

Speaker: NM
Manx: V'ad err jannoo shen kiart dy liooar.
English: They were err, doing that right enough.
